retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Liver-function tests

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency include: Asthma Emphysema Chronic bronchitis Chronic liver disease

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Etiology -- Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Phenotypes
retrieved_docs.content: Normal is associated with normal levels of AAT and its proper functioning. The group of normal alleles is referred to as M, and the typical genotype with normal AAT levels is MM.

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Etiology -- Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Phenotypes
retrieved_docs.content: Null alleles result in the absence of detectable AAT protein in the plasma. Therefore, individuals with the null genotype are less common and are at higher risk for the most severe form of lung disease, while liver disease is not typically associated with null alleles.

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Etiology -- Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Phenotypes
retrieved_docs.content: Dysfunctional alleles are characterized by producing a normal quantity of AAT protein, but the protein fails to function properly. This dysfunction can be attributed to either a conversion defect from an elastase inhibitor to a thrombin inhibitor or the presence of the PI*F variant. [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Etiology -- Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Phenotypes
retrieved_docs.content: Deficient is associated with a deficiency in plasma AAT levels below 35% of the average normal level. Homozygotes with AAT deficiency typically have 5 to 6 μmol/L plasma levels. The most common deficient allele associated with emphysema is the Z allele, which is present in approximately 2% to 3% of the white population in the United States.

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT) deficiency is a genetically inherited disorder often unrecognized in clinical practice. It results in the impaired production of alpha-1 antitrypsin protein, which plays a role in protecting the body from neutrophil elastase, an enzyme released by white blood cells during infection. Due to defective protein production, there is reduced activity of AAT in the blood and lungs. Additionally, abnormal AAT levels can lead to the accumulation of AAT in the liver, leading to liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Optimal management of AAT deficiency requires the collaboration of an interprofessional team of healthcare professionals, including a pediatrician, geneticist, pulmonologist, gastroenterologist, and internist. Regular monitoring of lung function, including FEV 1 , and periodic chest computed tomography scans are essential for assessing disease progression and guiding treatment decisions. Individualized management plans should be developed based on the patient's specific needs, risk factors, and disease progression to preserve lung function and optimize long-term outcomes

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Etiology -- Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Genetics
retrieved_docs.content: Worldwide, it is estimated that 161 million people carry 1 copy of the S or Z allele and 1 copy of the M allele in each cell (MS or MZ). Individuals with an MS (or SS) combination typically produce sufficient amounts of alpha-1 antitrypsin to protect their lungs. However, people carrying MZ alleles have a slightly increased risk of experiencing impaired lung or liver function.

retrieved_docs.content: Medical history and physical examination

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Etiology -- Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Phenotypes
retrieved_docs.content: AAT phenotypes are based on the electrophoretic mobility of the proteins produced by the various abnormal AAT alleles. Genotyping is performed by identifying specific alleles in deoxyribonucleic acid. Based on this, variants of AAT can be categorized into 4 basic groups:

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The activity provides participants with comprehensive knowledge of the evaluation and management of AAT deficiency, including its etiology, clinical manifestations, and diagnostic approaches. By understanding the disease's complexity, participants learn to identify and differentiate AAT deficiency from other conditions, screen at-risk populations, and implement effective treatment strategies. Collaboration with an interprofessional team—including pulmonologists, hepatologists, and genetic counselors—enhances patient care by ensuring a multidisciplinary approach when diagnosing and managing AAT deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Confirm AAT deficiency: Severe AAT deficiency is diagnosed by confirming a serum level below 11 μmols/L (approximately 57 mg/dL by nephelometry), along with the presence of a severely deficient genotype identified through testing for the most common defective alleles (ie, S, Z, I, F).

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Genotyping of the protease inhibitor (Pi) locus is performed on a blood sample using polymerase chain reaction (PCR) technology or restriction fragment length polymorphisms. These tests detect the most commonly known variants (F, I, S, Z). In cases where these tests fail to determine the genetic variant, gene sequencing of exonic DNA can be employed as an alternative method.

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Mutation(Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Studies have shown that the most commonly found deficiency allele in alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency is the PI*Z allele, which is more prevalent in northern and western Europe. In comparison, PI*S allele variants have a higher prevalence in southern Europe. The prevalence of the PI*ZZ deficiency allele accounts for 0.1% of the world population, while PI*SZ heterozygous allele deficiency represents about 0.7% of deficiency genotypes worldwide. Despite the relatively common appearance of these deficiency alleles, AATD is highly underdiagnosed, with fewer than 10% of the expected number of cases reported in the United States. The mean age when the disease was diagnosed was 41.3 years. As with other rare diseases, it often takes several doctors and many years before a diagnosis is made. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: neonatal period, anchors a spectrum of early‑life disorders such as hereditary metabolic disease (Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome), multiple jaundice subtypes (neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation of bilirubin, breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic neonatal jaundice, hemolytic jaundice, perinatal jaundice, Rh‑alloimmunization‑related jaundice, prematurity‑associated jaundice), and hemorrhagic presentations (general neonatal hemorrhage, intracranial neonatal hemorrhage, umbilical bleeding after birth, respiratory‑tract bleeding). These hemorrhagic events are linked to an unspecified perinatal condition that may arise from perinatal infections, which include unspecified bacterial sepsis of the newborn and neonatal botulism. Laboratory findings described as “Below reference range” are interpreted in the context of hemolytic and perinatal jaundice, providing quantitative guidance for diagnosis. The disorder “Disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight” is both a child of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 150
retrieved_docs.content: (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان); and * Jaundice specifically caused by Rh alloimmunization in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). These jaundice subtypes are often evaluated together with total hemoglobin concentration, where values below the reference range support the diagnosis of hemolytic disease. Recognizing the interrelated nature of neonatal infections, genetic syndromes, hemolytic processes, diverse jaundice mechanisms and hemorrhagic events—along with appropriate laboratory interpretation—is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely intervention and optimal care of affected newborns.

Laboratory findings that fall below reference ranges, particularly low total hemoglobin concentration, are interpreted as indicative of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice, underscoring the diagnostic importance of quantitative testing in this age group. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the neonatal period serves as a critical temporal nexus linking infectious, metabolic, hematologic, and hemorrhagic conditions—many organized under “other perinatal conditions” or “hemolytic‑jaundice” categories—and that recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma and DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) are both classified as specific forms of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, the broader category of neonatal disorders. The condition Cerebral injury due to birth trauma represents a narrower, trauma‑related neurological injury that occurs in the newborn period, while transient neonatal diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder that appears shortly after birth and typically resolves. Recognizing that both entities fall under the umbrella of neonatal disorders highlights the need for targeted assessment and management strategies tailored to the unique pathophysiology of each condition in newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 917
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified under neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This broader category encompasses several specific neonatal conditions, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical hemorrhage after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (respiratory tract hemorrhage in the newborn), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice). Together, these entities represent key hemorrhagic and hyperbilirubinemic presentations that are important for the diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected newborns.

Finally, the Neonatal Disorders SMQ includes the condition “unspecified perinatal infection” (عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك) to ensure that any unclassified infection around birth is captured within this neonatal disorder grouping.
